metadata.summary: Systematic methodology for discovering and scoring customer pain points across public sources using a 5-weighted-factor matrix, converting qualitative signal into a comparable priority list.

content.title: Pain Point Research
content.summary: A systematic methodology for discovering and scoring customer pain points across public sources (Reddit, G2, App Store, Quora, Upwork). The rule: define audience + context first, mine at least 3 source tiers, capture verbatim quotes with URLs, score each pain on 5 weighted factors (Frequency 30%, Severity 25%, Reach 20%, Spend 15%, Alternatives 10%), then extract root causes via 5-Whys for the top findings.

why.p: Founders who skip structured pain-point research either miss the real problem or build for the wrong audience. Surface-level scanning of a single source produces survivorship bias and outrage artifacts. The scoring matrix converts subjective gut-feel into a comparable priority list, and 5-Whys prevents building for first-order symptoms instead of root causes.

list.item: Pre-MVP: target audience defined but no validated problem yet.
Choosing between 3-5 candidate ideas — score the underlying pains to break ties.
Exploring an unfamiliar niche where domain intuition is weak.
Repurposing existing research (G2 reviews, Reddit threads) into a structured opportunity backlog.
You already have paying users — switch to user interviews and cancellation post-mortems; stranger pain points add noise.
Highly regulated domains (healthcare, finance) where complaints are NDA'd and public signal is misleading.
B2B enterprise where buyers don't post complaints publicly — use expert calls and analyst reports instead.
Quantitative effect sizes are required — this methodology yields qualitative signal only.

rule.statement: Define audience, context, and domain before searching; never begin mining without a written scope statement.
rule.rationale: Vague scope produces unactionable findings. "Founders" is too broad; "solo founders, US, post-launch B2B SaaS, under $10k MRR" produces searchable, comparable pain points.

example.title: Proper scope definition
example.code: Audience: Freelance web developers Context: Client project management Domain: Remote services Goal: Find 20 pain points in 4 hours

section.name: source-tiers
section.p: Tier 1 (highest signal): direct complaints — Reddit sorted by top/controversial, Twitter/X rants, Product Hunt negative reviews, G2/Capterra 1-3 star reviews, App Store 1-2 star reviews.
Tier 2 (problem indicators): questions — Quora "how to" queries, Stack Overflow duplicates, subreddit FAQ sections, Google "People Also Ask" results.
Tier 3 (communities): Slack groups, Discord servers, Facebook groups, Indie Hackers discussions.
Tier 4 (jobs and gigs): Upwork job posts, Fiverr popular gigs, LinkedIn job listings for "looking for someone to...".

rule.statement: Mine at least 3 source tiers per research session; never rely on a single source type.
rule.rationale: Reddit alone is biased toward complainers. Mixing Tier 1 complaints with Tier 2 questions and Tier 4 gigs triangulates the real pain landscape.

rule.statement: Capture the verbatim quote and URL for every pain point; never substitute an agent's paraphrase.
rule.rationale: Paraphrases lose signal and cannot be re-validated. Every quote must link to an actual URL or it is worthless for downstream validation.

section.name: pain-categories
section.p: Classify each pain point into one of eight categories:

list.item: Time Waste — takes too long to do X (example: "spend 2 hours updating reports").
Money Loss — costs more than it should (example: "paying $200/mo for features I don't use").
Complexity — too hard to understand or configure.
Integration — tools don't work together; copy-paste between apps.
Reliability — doesn't work consistently; crashes regularly.
Support — can't get help; no response from support.
Process — workflow is broken; too many approvals for simple changes.
Learning — hard to get started; long setup time.

rule.statement: Score every pain point using: Score = (Frequency × 0.30) + (Severity × 0.25) + (Reach × 0.20) + (Spend × 0.15) + (Alternatives × 0.10). Scale each factor 1-5.
rule.rationale: The weighted formula prevents single-factor inflation. Frequency dominates because a pain that affects users daily is a stronger product anchor than a catastrophic but rare pain.

list.item: Frequency (30%): 1 = rarely, 3 = weekly, 5 = daily.
Severity (25%): 1 = annoying, 3 = painful, 5 = blocking.
Reach (20%): 1 = few people, 3 = many, 5 = everyone in defined audience.
Spend (15%): 1 = $0 spent solving it, 3 = some spend, 5 = significant spend.
Alternatives (10%): 1 = many alternatives exist, 3 = few, 5 = none.

rule.statement: Priority thresholds: score above 4 = critical (high priority), 3-4 = significant (worth solving), 2-3 = moderate (could be a feature), below 2 = minor (deprioritize).
rule.rationale: Clear numeric thresholds prevent subjective override of the scoring. A score of 3.8 is not "basically a 4"; it is significant but not critical.

rule.statement: Run 5-Whys analysis for the top 3 scored pain points; the first-order pain is rarely the right thing to build.
rule.rationale: Building for first-order symptoms produces features, not products. Root causes reveal structural opportunities that competitors also miss.

example.title: 5-Whys analysis
example.code: Pain: "I spend 3 hours/week on expense reports" Why 1: Reports are manual Why 2: System does not auto-import receipts Why 3: No OCR integration Why 4: IT will not approve new tools Why 5: Tool approval process takes 6 months Root cause: Organizational friction in tool adoption

antipattern.description: Inflating Reach to 5 ("everyone has this") without three distinct sources mentioning it.
antipattern.reason: Complaints are loud and unrepresentative. Cap Reach at 4 unless multiple independent sources confirm broad prevalence.

antipattern.description: Splitting scoring across multiple agent calls, each with a fresh context window.
antipattern.reason: The scoring rubric drifts between calls; item 40 gets a different standard than item 1. Score the full batch in one agent call with the rubric in the system prompt.

antipattern.description: Mining only one source type (e.g., only Reddit) and treating results as representative.
antipattern.reason: Reddit skews toward complainers and outliers. The "fine but mediocre" majority — where most opportunity lives — doesn't post there.

how-to.step: Write a scope statement: audience, context, domain, and research goal (e.g., "find 20 pain points in 4 hours").
Identify 3-4 sources per tier: Tier 1 (Reddit, G2, App Store), Tier 2 (Quora, Stack Overflow), Tier 3 (Discord, Slack), Tier 4 (Upwork, Fiverr).
For each source, search using keywords related to the audience and context; save at least 20 raw results (posts, reviews, comments).
Extract pain points as verbatim quotes with URLs. Each quote must link to an actual source (no paraphrases).
Categorize each quote into one of the 8 pain categories.
Score each pain point using the 5-factor matrix (Frequency, Severity, Reach, Spend, Alternatives), 1-5 each, in a single batch.
Sort by final score (descending) and identify the top 10 pain points.
Run 5-Whys analysis for the top 3, drilling from first-order pain to root cause.
Extract 2-3 business angles from each root cause (e.g., direct solution, integration play, process reform).
Pair with problem-validation interviews to test the top pain points with real users.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Run as a 3-stage pipeline. Stage 1 (haiku): mine sources in parallel — one agent per Tier source (Reddit, G2, App Store, Quora, Upwork). Each returns a JSON list of {quote, url, source, raw_category_guess}. Stage 2 (sonnet): a single dedupe + categorize agent merges, removes near-duplicates, assigns one of the 8 categories, and applies the scoring rubric. Stage 3 (opus): an opportunity-extraction agent reads the top-N scored points, runs 5-Whys, and proposes business angles. Human reviews stage-2 categories and stage-3 angles before any further work.

list.item: Define audience + context BEFORE searching. "Founders" is too broad; "Solo founders, US, post-launch B2B SaaS, <$10k MRR" is searchable.
Capture the verbatim quote and URL — never the agent's paraphrase. Paraphrases lose signal and can't be re-validated.
Use ≥3 source tiers. Reddit alone is biased toward complainers; mix with Quora questions and Upwork gigs to triangulate.
Score in a single sitting with a single agent — splitting scoring across calls drifts on the rubric.
Re-run quarterly for the same audience; pain-point landscape shifts (LLMs killed many "I spent 2 hours on X" complaints in 2024).
Always extract root cause via 5-Whys for the top 3. The first-order pain is rarely the right thing to build.
Pair with problem-validation and jobs-to-be-done — a high-score pain that fails JTBD-fit is a feature, not a product.
LLMs invent plausible Reddit quotes when given a topic without raw text input. Always pass scraped text into context, not just a topic; require URLs.
Categorization drifts mid-batch (the model "decides" Time-Waste means something different by item 40). Use one explicit category list in every system prompt.
Models inflate Reach scores ("everyone has this") because complaints are loud. Cap Reach at 4 unless ≥3 distinct sources mention it.
Cross-language pain points: a UA-language Reddit thread is invisible to most agents. Use a translator agent in stage 1 explicitly.
Human checkpoint: opportunities from stage 3 should be reviewed before any go-to-market work — opus output is plausible but unvalidated; treat as hypotheses.
Don't let the agent "summarize" — keep raw quotes in the log. Summaries lose the verbatim signal needed for downstream JTBD interviews.
Never let the same agent both score AND extract opportunities; opportunity-pull biases scoring.
Survivorship and outrage bias: 1-star reviews skew loud edge cases, missing the "fine but mediocre" majority where most opportunity lives.
Score weights (Frequency 30% / Severity 25% / Reach 20% / Spend 15% / Alternatives 10%) are heuristic; agents quote them as if they were calibrated, which they aren't.
Reddit/Twitter scraping is increasingly rate-limited and ToS-restricted; agent pipelines that hit the API directly break or violate terms.
Without demographic context, "Reach 5 = everyone" inflates score for niche pain. Always anchor reach in the defined audience scope, not the internet.
LLMs hallucinate plausible-sounding quotes — every quote in the log MUST link back to an actual URL or it's worthless for follow-up validation.

template.purpose: Research log template: scope, PP-NNN entries with scores, summary by category, top-5 list.
Reddit-specific extraction template with posts analyzed, pain points found, notable quotes.
Python script: reads JSON list of pain points, computes weighted score, outputs CSV.
